title: Customer Directory
story: As a new data analyst, the first thing you need to do is explore our customer base. Retrieve all columns from the Customers table.
hints: Use the SELECT keyword followed by the columns you want.
The asterisk (*) is a wildcard that selects all columns.
Try running: SELECT * FROM Customers;

title: Product Catalog
story: The warehouse manager needs a list of all products. Retrieve all columns from the Products table.
hints: Similar to the previous lesson, use SELECT *.
Make sure you are targeting the Products table this time.
Try: SELECT * FROM Products;

title: Customer Contact List
story: The marketing team only needs customer names. Return only the first_name and last_name columns from Customers.
hints: Instead of *, list the columns separated by a comma.
Column names are first_name and last_name.
SELECT first_name, last_name FROM Customers;

title: Order Ledger
story: Let's look at what's been happening in sales. Retrieve all columns from the Orders table.
hints: Select everything from the Orders table.
The command structure is always SELECT ... FROM ...
SELECT * FROM Orders;

title: Employee Overview
story: Management wants a simple staff list. Return first_name, last_name, and department from the Employees table.
hints: Select the three specific columns requested.
Separate the column names with commas.
SELECT first_name, last_name, department FROM Employees;

title: Processing Orders
story: The logistics team needs to know which orders are still Processing. Filter the Orders table where the status is 'Processing'.
hints: Use the WHERE clause to filter rows.
String values like 'Processing' must be enclosed in single quotes.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Processing';

title: Electronics Inventory
story: Find all products that belong to the Electronics category.
hints: Filter the Products table.
Use WHERE category = 'Electronics'.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE category = 'Electronics';

title: High Value Orders
story: We need to track significant sales. Find all orders where the amount is greater than 500.
hints: Use the greater than operator (>).
Numbers should not have quotes.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE amount > 500;

title: Low Stock Alert
story: Alert the stock manager! Find products where the stock is less than 30.
hints: Use the less than operator (<).
Check the Products table.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE stock < 30;

title: Active Orders
story: Find all orders that have NOT been 'Delivered'.
hints: The 'not equal' operator is (<>).
Filter status in the Orders table.
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE status <> 'Delivered';

title: Newest Orders
story: The CEO wants to see our latest activity. Sort all orders by order_date in descending order (newest first).
hints: Add ORDER BY at the end of your query.
Use DESC for descending order.
SELECT * FROM Orders ORDER BY order_date DESC;

title: Top Expensive Products
story: Show me the Top 3 most expensive products in our catalog. Include only the name and price.
hints: Combine ORDER BY price DESC with the LIMIT clause.
Make sure you select only name and price.
SELECT name, price FROM Products ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 3;

title: Selected Customers
story: A special promotion is running for our first three customers. Find all orders placed by customer_id 1, 2, or 3.
hints: Instead of using multiple OR statements, use the IN keyword.
The syntax is WHERE column IN (val1, val2, ...).
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE customer_id IN (1, 2, 3);

title: Mid Range Products
story: Find products with a price between 50 and 500.
hints: The BETWEEN operator is inclusive.
Syntax is WHERE price BETWEEN 50 AND 500.
SELECT * FROM Products WHERE price BETWEEN 50 AND 500;

title: Preview Orders
story: Quick check! Return only the 5 most recent orders.
hints: First sort by date descending.
Then apply LIMIT 5.
SELECT * FROM Orders ORDER BY order_date DESC LIMIT 5;

title: Total Orders
story: How many total orders have been placed so far? Use COUNT.
hints: Use the COUNT(*) function.
Select this from the Orders table.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Orders;

title: Total Revenue
story: Calculate the SUM of all order amounts.
hints: Use the SUM() function and pass 'amount' as the argument.
SELECT SUM(amount) FROM Orders;
Remember that numbers might look different depending on seed data.

title: Average Product Price
story: What is the average price of all the products we sell?
hints: Use the AVG() function on the price column in the Products table.
SELECT AVG(price) FROM Products;
SQLite does not automatically round results unless you ask it to.

title: Orders Per Customer
story: Count how many orders each customer has placed. Show customer_id and the count.
hints: You need to use GROUP BY at the end of the query.
Group specifically by customer_id.
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id;

title: High Value Customers
story: Find customer_ids of people who have placed more than 1 order. Use HAVING.
hints: HAVING is used to filter groups whereas WHERE filters rows.
Use GROUP BY customer_id first, then HAVING COUNT(*) > 1.
SELECT customer_id FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

title: Customer Orders
story: We need the customer names for each order. JOIN the Customers and Orders tables. Show order id and customer first_name.
hints: Join on the common column: customer_id in Orders and id in Customers.
Use INNER JOIN or just JOIN.
SELECT Orders.id, Customers.first_name FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id;

title: Order Product Names
story: Which product was purchased in each order? JOIN Orders and Products to show order id and product name.
hints: Join on product_id in Orders and id in Products.
Orders is your main table.
SELECT Orders.id, Products.name FROM Orders JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Order Details Dashboard
story: The dashboard needs a detailed view. Show first_name (from Customers), product name, and order amount.
hints: You need to chain two JOIN statements.
JOIN Customers ON ... JOIN Products ON ...
SELECT Customers.first_name, Products.name, Orders.amount FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Customer Purchase History
story: Show a history of what each customer has bought. Select first_name and product name.
hints: Similar to the previous query, but only select two columns.
Chain JOINs on the Orders table.
SELECT Customers.first_name, Products.name FROM Orders JOIN Customers ON Orders.customer_id = Customers.id JOIN Products ON Orders.product_id = Products.id;

title: Potential Customers
story: We might have users who registered but never placed an order. Find the first_name of customers who have no entries in the Orders table.
hints: Use a LEFT JOIN from Customers to Orders.
Then filter for where the order ID is NULL.
SELECT Customers.first_name FROM Customers LEFT JOIN Orders ON Customers.id = Orders.customer_id WHERE Orders.id IS NULL;

title: Add a New Customer
story: We have a walk-in! Insert a new customer named Rahul Sharma with email r.sharma@example.com and today's date 2023-10-25.
hints: Use INSERT INTO Customers (first_name, last_name, email, registration_date) VALUES (...);
Strings must be in single quotes.
INSERT INTO Customers (first_name, last_name, email, registration_date) VALUES ('Rahul', 'Sharma', 'r.sharma@example.com', '2023-10-25');

title: New Product Arrival
story: New inventory! Insert a Gaming Headset in the Electronics category for 99.99 with 40 in stock.
hints: Use INSERT INTO Products.
List name, category, price, stock.
INSERT INTO Products (name, category, price, stock) VALUES ('Gaming Headset', 'Electronics', 99.99, 40);

title: Restock Inventory
story: A shipment of Laptops arrived. Update the stock of the Laptop to 50.
hints: Use the UPDATE table SET column = value WHERE condition syntax.
UPDATE Products SET stock = 50 WHERE name = 'Laptop';
Always include a WHERE clause to avoid updating all rows!

title: Correct Order Amount
story: Order 102 had a coupon we forgot to apply. Update its amount to 40.00.
hints: UPDATE Orders SET amount = 40.00 WHERE id = 102;
Filter by the order id.
SET amount = 40.00.

title: Remove Cancelled Orders
story: Clean up the database. Delete all orders that have a status of Cancelled.
hints: Use DELETE FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled';
Be very careful with DELETE, there is no undo!
DELETE FROM Orders WHERE status = 'Cancelled';

title: Email Prefix
story: Extract the first 4 characters of every customer's email.
hints: SQLite uses SUBSTR(column, start, length).
Note that indices start at 1.
SELECT SUBSTR(email, 1, 4) as email_prefix FROM Customers;

title: Find the @ Symbol
story: Determine the character position of the '@' symbol in each email.
hints: Use the INSTR(string, substring) function.
SELECT INSTR(email, '@') as at_position FROM Customers;
Searching for a single character.

title: Email Username
story: Extract the text *before* the '@' symbol in each email.
hints: Combine SUBSTR and INSTR.
Start at 1, and go for (INSTR(email, '@') - 1) characters.
SELECT SUBSTR(email, 1, INSTR(email, '@') - 1) as username FROM Customers;

title: Customer Tenure
story: How many days have passed since each customer registered? Use julianday.
hints: Use julianday('now') - julianday(registration_date).
The result depends on today's current date.
SELECT first_name, (julianday('2026-03-15') - julianday(registration_date)) as days_registered FROM Customers;

title: Customer Email Domain
story: Extract the email domain (everything after the '@').
hints: Start at INSTR(email, '@') + 1.
You don't need a length argument for SUBSTR to go to the end.
SELECT SUBSTR(email, INSTR(email, '@') + 1) as domain FROM Customers;

title: Rank Products by Price
story: Rank our products from most expensive to cheapest. Show name, price, and rank.
hints: Use the RANK() window function.
OVER(ORDER BY price DESC).
SELECT name, price, RANK() OVER(ORDER BY price DESC) as price_rank FROM Products;

title: Employee Salary Ranking
story: Rank employees based on their salary across the whole company.
hints: Use RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC).
Select from the Employees table.
SELECT first_name, salary, RANK() OVER(ORDER BY salary DESC) as salary_rank FROM Employees;

title: Department Salary Leaderboard
story: Rank employees by salary within their own departments.
hints: You need PARTITION BY inside the OVER clause.
RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC).
SELECT first_name, department, salary, RANK() OVER(PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) as dept_rank FROM Employees;

title: Top Customers by Spending
story: Find the top spending customers. First calculate total spending per customer in a CTE, then rank them.
hints: Define the CTE with WITH spend_table AS (...).
In the main query, select from your spend_table and use RANK().

WITH CustomerSpend AS (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) as total_spent FROM Orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT customer_id, total_spent, RANK() OVER(ORDER BY total_spent DESC) as rank FROM CustomerSpend;

title: Running Revenue
story: Calculate the cumulative (running) total of revenue as it grows day by day.
hints: Use SUM(amount) OVER(ORDER BY order_date).
Window functions can perform cumulative sums.
SELECT order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER(ORDER BY order_date) as running_total FROM Orders;
